paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 16: Three Fish from Tekka (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Sec 3 girl who accompanies her father to Tekka wet market early on a Sunday morning to buy fish for her dying grandmother, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
At half past six on Sunday morning, Tekka was already loud. My father walked one step ahead of me, his canvas bag swinging at his side, and I followed him through the narrow aisles the way I had followed him since I was in Primary 1. The difference was that this morning we were buying fish for Ah Ma, who had not eaten in three days.

Paragraph 2
Ah Ma was in Ward 7B at the hospital. The doctors had stopped using the word 'recovery' a week ago. They used softer words now — comfort, manage, quality. My father and I had been sent to do what families in our part of Singapore have always done: buy something fresh, cook it gently, and bring it. As if a steamed fish could fix what the hospital could not.

Paragraph 3
We stopped at Auntie Goh's stall near the back. Auntie Goh had been selling fish to our family for fifteen years. She knew that Ah Ma had, for as long as I could remember, eaten only the white belly of a particular type of red snapper, which Auntie Goh kept under a separate sheet of ice for our family.

Paragraph 4
My father pointed at two snappers. 'These two, please,' he said. His voice was lower than usual. Auntie Goh nodded once, did not ask why we had not come last Sunday, did not ask after Ah Ma. She weighed the two fish and slid them into a clear plastic bag. Then, without a word, she reached under the second sheet of ice, lifted out a third smaller snapper, and dropped it into the bag.

Paragraph 5
My father opened his wallet. Auntie Goh shook her head and waved his hand away. 'For your Ah Ma,' she said. 'I know lah.' That was all. She turned to serve the next customer before my father could protest. I stood frozen with the styrofoam box at my feet, my cheeks suddenly hot, embarrassed that I did not know how to behave in the face of a kindness so plainly given.

Paragraph 6
We walked out into the morning light. The noodle uncle at the corner stall lifted his ladle in a small wave at my father — not the usual cheerful shout, just a small nod, as though he too had been told to be gentle. The prata auntie behind her hot griddle looked up, met my father's eyes for one long second, and then looked down again without smiling.

Paragraph 7
On the 65 bus home, I sat with the styrofoam box between my feet, the three fish sliding with every brake. Slowly I began to understand. The man who sold us banana leaves, the way he had given my father an extra leaf 'for luck'. The pharmacist auntie who had refused to charge for an extra bottle of saline. The kway teow uncle who had quietly added one extra prawn to our bowls every Saturday for a month. None of it had been coincidence. They had all known. They had been kind in small unspoken ways for weeks, and we had been too tired to notice.

Paragraph 8
When we reached our void deck in Tampines, my father set the box down. 'We will steam all three,' he said. 'Ah Ma always said three is a luckier number than two.' His voice was steady, but he did not look at me when he spoke. I picked up the box. The ice was half-melted, but the fish were still cold, and the morning, somehow, did not feel as heavy as it had at the market gates.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Tekka: a wet market in Little India; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block; prata: a South Indian griddle bread popular in Singapore.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what details make it clear that the narrator and her father are very familiar with Tekka market? [1]
questions.answer: her father walks one step ahead of her with his canvas bag swinging, and she follows him through the narrow aisles the way she has done since she was in Primary 1 — suggesting they have made this trip together for many years

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, why does the narrator say that the doctors now use 'softer words' such as comfort, manage and quality? [1]
questions.answer: these gentler words signal that the doctors no longer expect Ah Ma to recover — they are preparing the family for her death by avoiding the harsher reality directly

questions.stem: Q3. What is the effect of the sentence 'As if a steamed fish could fix what the hospital could not' (Paragraph 2)? [1]
questions.answer: it gently highlights the helplessness of the family — they know a fish cannot cure Ah Ma, but it is the only meaningful thing they can do, capturing the painful gap between modern medicine and traditional acts of love

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 3, what does the detail that Auntie Goh keeps a special type of red snapper 'under a separate sheet of ice' for the narrator's family show? [1]
questions.answer: Auntie Goh has a long-standing personal relationship with the family — she sets aside the particular fish Ah Ma likes especially for them, going beyond ordinary service to look after them

questions.stem: Q5(a). Why did Auntie Goh not ask after Ah Ma or why the family had not come last Sunday (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: she already knew that Ah Ma was very ill and did not want to press the family or make them say it aloud — her silence was a form of tact and respect

questions.stem: Q5(b). What is the significance of Auntie Goh quietly adding a third smaller snapper to the bag (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: it is a small unspoken gift to the family — Auntie Goh is offering an extra fish out of compassion, without making it into a public gesture or asking the family to thank her

questions.stem: Q6. Why did the narrator's cheeks become 'suddenly hot' when Auntie Goh refused payment (Paragraph 5)? [1]
questions.answer: she was embarrassed because she did not know how to respond to such an open act of kindness — it caught her off guard and she felt awkward standing there with the styrofoam box

questions.stem: Q7. What does the noodle uncle's small wave 'not the usual cheerful shout' (Paragraph 6) suggest about him? [1]
questions.answer: he too knew about Ah Ma's condition and was deliberately quieter than usual, replacing his normal noisy greeting with a gentle nod out of respect for what the family was going through

questions.stem: Q8. What does the word 'frozen' suggest about how the narrator felt at Auntie Goh's stall (Paragraph 5)? [1]
questions.answer: she was so taken aback by the kindness that she could not move or react — she was momentarily paralysed by the emotional weight of what had just happened

questions.stem: Q9. In Paragraph 7, what does the narrator finally come to understand on the bus home? [1]
questions.answer: she realises that many people around them — the banana-leaf seller, the pharmacist auntie, the kway teow uncle — had quietly known about Ah Ma for weeks and had been showing kindness in small unspoken ways that the family had been too tired and distracted to notice

questions.stem: Q10(a). Why did the narrator's father avoid looking at her when he said 'three is a luckier number than two' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: he was trying to keep his voice steady and did not trust himself to meet her eyes without showing his own grief — looking away helped him hold himself together

questions.stem: Q10(b). What is the effect of ending the passage with 'the morning, somehow, did not feel as heavy as it had at the market gates' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests that although the situation has not changed, the quiet kindness shown by Auntie Goh and others has lightened the family's emotional burden — the narrator now feels supported by an unseen community of care

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as the Sunday morning unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: dutiful / bored / embarrassed / suspicious / moved / annoyed / grateful / triumphant. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Three Fish from Tekka' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the course of the morning — from a quiet sense of duty as she follows her father into the market for fish for Ah Ma, to embarrassment when Auntie Goh refuses payment and adds a third fish, to being deeply moved on the bus home as she realises how many neighbours had been showing quiet kindness for weeks, ending in a quiet gratitude back at the void deck. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 1–2, as she follows her father into Tekka at half past six on Sunday morning to buy fish for Ah Ma? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5, as she stands frozen with the styrofoam box at her feet after Auntie Goh waves her father's payment away and adds a third snapper to the bag? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7, on the 65 bus home as she realises that the banana-leaf seller, the pharmacist auntie, and the kway teow uncle had all been quietly kind to the family for weeks? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling at the end of Paragraph 8, back at the void deck in Tampines as she lifts the styrofoam box and notices that the morning no longer feels as heavy as it did at the market gates? [1]
